coredata.dc:title: Discrimination of normal/cancer cells in bioimaging through a rolling circle amplification-enhanced red carbon dots-embedded multivalent aptamers nanoplatform
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ning, Gan
Wang, Fang
Du, Huan
Zhang, Ruyan
Huo, Xiaobing
Wang, Xiufeng
Zhou, Ting
Zhang, Guodong
Zhang, Zhiqing
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) is a key biomarker closely associated with cancer, and its content varies greatly between normal cells and cancer cells. However, intracellular detection of GSH was challenging because existing probes not only have a long detection time but also have fluorescence in the blue-green region that overlaps with the biological matrix's spontaneous fluorescence, thus affecting the detection accuracy. Therefore, a new red fluorescent nano-probe was needed to rapidly and accurately detected GSH within the biological matrix. Herein, red carbon dots (R-CDs) synthesized via hydrothermal method using N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and 4-Bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene as precursors offer enhanced fluorescence that could be quenched by MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NS) and restored by GSH. By combining R-CDs with the AS1411 aptamer and using rolling circle amplification, a multivalent aptamer modified R-CDs assembly (Assembly@R-CDs) was created for swift cancer cell targeting. Compared to monomeric aptamer, such multivalent aptamers exhibited higher affinity and selectivity, thereby enhancing the specificity and sensitivity of detection. After the fluorescence of the multivalent assembly was quenched by MnO2 NS (Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS), it could be restored when targeting cancer cells, which could realize the distinction between normal cells and cancer cells. The experiment showed that 4T1 cancer cells took up more Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS than L929 normal cells and generated stronger fluorescence, indicating the high selectivity for cancer cell detection. The potential of such nanosystem for tumor diagnosis combination therapy is promising, especially considering the embedding properties of anthracene drugs such as doxorubicin in DNA carriers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rolling circle amplification
GSH
Aptamer
Cell imaging
Cell recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs and MnO2 NS
Synthesis of AS1411 aptamer modified R-CDs (AS1411@R-CDs)
Characterizations of R-CDs and MnO2 NS
Feasibility of determining GSH using the R-CDs@MnO2 NS nanocomposites
Assessing the feasibility of cellular GSH detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022MB148 and ZR2023MB148) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (22CX03024A, 23CX03005A and 23CX03006A).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterizations of R-CDs. (A) The synthesis process of R-CDs; (B) TEM image of R-CDs; (C) Particle size distribution of R-CDs; (D) XRD pattern of R-CDs; (E) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs; (F) XPS spectra of R-CDs, (G) Br 3d, (H) C1s, (I) N1s and (J) O1s; (K) UV–vis absorption (Abs), (K) UV-vis absorption (Abs), FL emission (Em) and excitation (Ex) spectra of R-CDs (inset: photograph of R-CDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right)).

Characterization and analysis. (A) UV–vis spectra of the R-CDs, MnO2 NS, and R-CDs@MnO2 NS; Emission and excitation spectrum of R-CDs; (B) Fluorescence decay traces of R-CDs and R-CDs@MnO2 NS; (C) Zeta potential of R-CDs, MnO2 NS, and R-CDs@MnO2 NS; TEM of MnO2 NS (D), R-CDs@MnO2 NS (E), and R-CDs@MnO2 NS after adding GSH (F); Size distribution plot of (G) MnO2 NS, (H) R-CDs@MnO2 NS, (I) R-CDs@MnO2 NS after adding GSH.

Detection of GSH by R-CDs@MnO2 NS and the anti-interference determination. (A) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs@MnO2 NS (0–800 μM); (B) Relationship between the F/F0 value and the concentration of GSH with different concentrations of GSH (0–800 μM). Inset: Linear correlation between the F/F0 value and the concentration of GSH (0.5–120 μM). Here, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs and R-CDs@MnO2 NS in the presence of GSH, respectively; (C) Detection selectivity of R-CDs@MnO2 NS. Concentrations of GSH and interference substances were 500 μM and 1 mM, respectively. Here, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs and R-CDs@MnO2 NS in the presence of material, respectively; (D) Influences of potential interferents on the detection of GSH using R-CDs@MnO2 NS. Concentrations of GSH and interference substances were 500 μM and 1 mM, respectively. Here, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs and R-CDs@MnO2 NS in the presence of GSH and material (M), respectively. (Ascorbic acid (AA), l-Tyrosine (L-Try), L-a-Alanine (L-a-Ala), L-Argininehydrochloride (L-Arg), l-Aspartic acid (L-Asp), l-Cysteine (L-Cys), l-Glutamic acid (L-Glu), l-Glycine (L-Gly), l-Histidine (L-His), l- Isoleucine (L- Isoleu), l-Leucine (L-Leu), l-Lysine (L-Lys), l-Methionine (L-Met), l-Proline (L-Pro), l-Serine (L-Ser), l-Threonine (L-Thr), L-Tryptophan (L-Try), l-Phenylalanine (L-Phe), l-Asparagine monohydrate (L-Asp).); R-CDs (50 μg/mL), MnO2 NS (25 μg/mL).

Assembly@R-CDs specific target 4T1 cells. (A) FL microscopy images of L929 and 4T1 cells incubated with AS1411@R-CDs for different time intervals; (B) Flow cytometry images of L929 and 4T1 cells incubated with Assembly@R-CDs for different durations; (C) ImageJ analysis of R-CDs signal in (A); (D) FL microscopic images and corresponding flow cytometry images of L929 and 4T1 cells, respectively, after 30 min of incubation with R-CDs, AS1411@R-CDs and Assembly@R-CDs. Scale bars: 20 μm.

(A) The FL images of 4T1 cells treated with different concentrations of NEM for 30 min and incubated with Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS; (B) The FL images depicting the effects of varying GSH concentrations on 4T1 cells previously treated with NEM (500 μM) and incubated with Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS. Scale bars: 20 μm.

Cancer cell recognition. The fluorescence (A) and flow cytometry (B) images of L929 and 4T1 cells incubated with Assembly@R-CDs and Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS for different durations, where AR and ARM represent Assembly@R-CDs and Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS, respectively. Scale bars: 20 μm.
Schematic illustration of preparation of Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS and the mechanism of recognizing cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Discrimination of normal/cancer cells in bioimaging through a rolling circle amplification-enhanced red carbon dots-embedded multivalent aptamers nanoplatform

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) is a key biomarker closely associated with cancer, and its content varies greatly between normal cells and cancer cells. However, intracellular detection of GSH was challenging because existing probes not only have a long detection time but also have fluorescence in the blue-green region that overlaps with the biological matrix's spontaneous fluorescence, thus affecting the detection accuracy. Therefore, a new red fluorescent nano-probe was needed to rapidly and accurately detected GSH within the biological matrix. Herein, red carbon dots (R-CDs) synthesized via hydrothermal method using N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide and 4-Bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene as precursors offer enhanced fluorescence that could be quenched by MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NS) and restored by GSH. By combining R-CDs with the AS1411 aptamer and using rolling circle amplification, a multivalent aptamer modified R-CDs assembly (Assembly@R-CDs) was created for swift cancer cell targeting. Compared to monomeric aptamer, such multivalent aptamers exhibited higher affinity and selectivity, thereby enhancing the specificity and sensitivity of detection. After the fluorescence of the multivalent assembly was quenched by MnO2 NS (Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS), it could be restored when targeting cancer cells, which could realize the distinction between normal cells and cancer cells. The experiment showed that 4T1 cancer cells took up more Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS than L929 normal cells and generated stronger fluorescence, indicating the high selectivity for cancer cell detection. The potential of such nanosystem for tumor diagnosis combination therapy is promising, especially considering the embedding properties of anthracene drugs such as doxorubicin in DNA carriers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a key antioxidant in cells, with levels from 1 to 10 mM, crucial for cell functions like metabolism and gene regulation [1,2]. Abnormal GSH levels are linked to diseases like cancer and diabetes [1–3]. Existing methods to measure GSH, such as Electrochemiluminescence [4], Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5], Fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometry [1], Liquid chromatography [6] and Raman spectroscopy [7], are reliable but have drawbacks like complex sample prep, high costs, and being time-consuming. There's a need for a simple, efficient, and cost-effective GSH detection method suitable for biological samples.

Carbon dots (CDs), which are novel carbon-based nanomaterials with a spherical structure and a particle size smaller than 10 nm, were first identified by Xu et al., in 2004 [8]. CDs are nanomaterials known for their low cost, ease of modification, and wide applicability in fields like biosensing and drug delivery due to their biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [9–13]. They have been used to detect substances like GSH in cells. For example, Gu et al. [14] used CDs synthesized from honey to detect GSH levels in MCF-7 cells, while Xie et al. [15] monitored biothiols with gold carbon dots (GCDs). MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NS) have also shown promise for biosensing due to their interaction with CDs, enhancing detection selectivity and sensitivity [16,17]. This was due to their unique properties that facilitate efficient interactions with other molecules, such as CDs. Specifically, the interaction between MnO2 NS and CDs created a synergistic effect that greatly enhanced the selectivity and sensitivity of detecting GSH [18]. The mechanism behind this enhancement involved the strong absorption capabilities of MnO2 NS in the ultraviolet to visible light range, which allowed them to act as efficient quenchers of the fluorescence emitted by CDs [19]. Moreover, GSH triggered redox reactions with MnO2 NS, leading to their reduction and the disassembly of the CDs@MnO2 complex when GSH was present. This disassembly restored the fluorescence of CDs, providing a quantitative readout that correlated with the GSH concentration in the sample. The development of such a sensitive and selective detection method held great potential for various applications, including the diagnosis of diseases related to oxidative stress, the screening of drugs that modulate GSH levels, and environmental monitoring for the presence of reactive species [20–22]. However, most CDs emitted blue to orange light, which was prone to interference from body fluorescence, reducing detection accuracy [18,23,24]. Red carbon dots (R-CDs) with emission wavelengths over 600 nm offered better tissue penetration for biomedical applications [11,25]. But most R-CDs were synthesized by solvothermal methods that were complex, time-consuming and costly, usually resulting in R-CDs with emission wavelengths shorter than 650 nm [10,26–28]. Since CDs with emission wavelengths of 650 nm or above are more important in biomedical applications, this is a significant limitation. Longer emission wavelengths provide enhanced tissue penetration depth for detailed and accurate deep tissue imaging, crucial for detecting diseases and monitoring treatment progress. Moreover, CDs emitting at 650 nm may have advantages like higher stability, lower toxicity and better compatibility with biological systems [25]. Therefore, there was an urgent need to develop a straightforward and reliable synthesis method for R-CDs with emission wavelengths greater than 650 nm for detecting GSH in biological matrices.

As reported, nanoparticulate systems could selectively accumulate at tumor sites through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, which occurred after prolonged blood circulation [29]. This phenomenon was due to the leaky vasculature and poor lymphatic drainage within tumors, allowing nanoparticles to penetrate and remain in the tumor tissue more effectively than in normal tissues. Despite the potential of the EPR effect for passive targeting, these strategies faced significant challenges, such as tumor microenvironment heterogeneity and limited vessel permeability, which hindered effective drug delivery [30]. To address these issues, researchers have developed active targeting approaches. By modifying nanoparticles with specific ligands, such as folic acid, aptamers, and antibodies, they enhanced the binding affinity and accumulation of nanoparticles at tumor sites [30,31]. Among them, the AS1411 aptamer showed extremely high affinity for nucleolin expressed on breast cancer cells, kidney cancer cells and other adenocarcinoma cells. This high affinity enabled nanocarriers to achieve remarkable high targeting specificity when acting on these cancer cells. By tightly binding to nucleolin on the surface of cancer cells, the AS1411 aptamer was like an accurate navigation system, guiding nanocarriers to accurately reach target cancer cells without error, greatly improving the targeting and effectiveness of treatment [30]. Additionally, compared with short-chain monovalent aptamers that have limitations such as low affinity, poor stability, and short blood circulation half-life which hinder precise targeting, multivalent aptamers exhibited better binding capabilities to cancer cells by significantly increasing the affinity and specificity of nanoparticle carriers for tumor cells and further optimizing their targeting accumulation at tumor sites [32,33]. At present, there is no report on the development of long-chain multivalent aptamer-nanodrug conjugates of R-CDs for efficient targeting of cancer cells to achieve effective recognition. Building on this, the rolling circle amplification (RCA) technique allowed the production of long single-stranded DNA that was complementary to the aptamer, offering the possibility to bind multiple aptamers and thereby form a multivalent aptamer system for efficient recognition of different types of tumor cells.

Inspired by the above situations, as described in Scheme 1 , R-CDs were fabricated via a one-step hydrothermal procedure, and their morphology, physicochemical properties, and optical properties were thoroughly characterized. Considering these characteristics, MnO2 NS were introduced to create an R-CDs@MnO2 NS nanocomposite material functioning as a fluorescent nanoprobe for GSH detection with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.01 μM. We further expanded this sensor platform for fluorescent detection of abnormally expressed GSH in cancer cells to differentiate between normal and cancer cells. AS1411 aptamer conjugated onto the positively charged surface of R-CDs through physical and chemical strategies to form AS1411@R-CDs and impart the complex with specific cell-targeting capabilities. To enhance its targeting capability, complementary long single-stranded DNA was synthesized by RCA method, which could combine with AS1411@R-CDs via base pairing, forming a multivalent aptamer system (Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS) for GSH detection in 4T1 cells. Its response to different concentrations of glutathione within cells also helped to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. These exceptional performances enabled the Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS nanoplatform to be used as a fluorescent nanoprobe for potential applications in cancer therapeutics diagnostics.
The details were provided in the “Supplementary information.”

The FL spectra were recorded with an F-7100 (HITACHI), UV–Vis spectra with a U–3900H (HITACHI), FT-IR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) spectrum with a Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific), XRD (X-ray diffraction) with ULTIMA IV (Rigaku), XPS (X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy) spectra with ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific), and time-resolved FL with a Horiba QM 8075 (Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH). TEM (Transmission Electron Microscope) images were observed with a JEM-1400Plus TEM (JEOL), size distribution with Nanobrook 90 plus PALS DLS (Brookhaven), and Zeta potential with Zeta Pals (Brookhaven). Cell imaging was done using IX73 Olympus microscopes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs and MnO2 NS
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process. 0.16 g N-(4-amino phenyl) acetamide (APAA) and 0.2 g 4-Bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene (4-Br-1,2-DAB) were dissolved in 20 mL water, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the crude product was centrifuged, filtered, and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h using a 500 Da membrane. The solution was then freeze-dried to yield R-CDs powder.

MnO2 NS was synthesized [24] by mixing 0.1 M H2SO4 (250 μL), 0.1 M SDS (5 mL), and 44.25 mL water, followed by heating at 95 °C for 15 min. After adding 0.05 M KMnO4 solution (500 μL), the reaction proceeded for an additional 90 min. The resulting black precipitate was collected, washed, and dried to yield MnO2 NS powder.

The QY of R-CDs was determined by using Rhodamine B (QY = 0.31, in water) as a reference [34,35]. Specifically, the concentrated solutions of Rhodamine B and R-CDs were diluted until their absorbance values were less than 0.05. The QY of R-CDs was calculated in accordance with the following formula:
Φ S
=
Φ R
F S
F R
A R
A S
n S
n R
Within the formula, the subscript R specifically designates Rhodamine B as the reference standard; the superscript S specifically designates R-CDs as the sample,
Φ
represents QY, F represents the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorption factor, and n refers to the refractive indices of the solvent.

Initially, 50 μL R-CDs (3 mg/mL) and 25 μL MnO2 NS (3 mg/mL) and 2925 μL PBS buffer were mixed evenly and reacted for 1 min. Subsequently, 10 μL of GSH with varying concentrations was introduced and the mixture was allowed to react for an additional 10 min at room temperature. Fluorescence detection was conducted immediately with excitation at 535 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AS1411 aptamer modified R-CDs (AS1411@R-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The grafting of AS1411 aptamer onto R-CDs was accomplished via electrostatic interaction and EDC/NHS activation [30]. In brief, R-CDs (5 mg) were incorporated into 930 μL ultrapure water. Subsequently, EDC (5 mg) and NHS (3 mg) were introduced into the aforementioned mixture and incubated at 37 °C for 0.5 h for activation. Thereafter, the AS1411 aptamer solution (70 μL, 100 μM) was added and kept in an incubator for 2 h. Then, the resulting solution was centrifuged and repeatedly washed multiple times with ultrapure water to eliminate excess reactants. Eventually, the AS1411@R-CDs was dispersed in ultrapure water for future utilization.

The circular DNA template was obtained through the ligation of the linear DNA template and a primer with T4 DNA ligase. Specifically, the linear DNA template (100 μM), T4 PNK enzyme (10 U/μL), T4 PNK enzyme buffer, and ATP (1 mM) were mixed and then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Next, it was heated at 90 °C for 5 min to inactivate the T4 PNK enzyme. Subsequently, the primer (100 μM) was added and incubated at 95 °C for 10 min. Then, it was slowly cooled to room temperature over approximately 3 h and left for another 3 h. After that, T4 DNA ligase (350 U/μL) and 10 × T4 DNA ligase buffer were added and incubated overnight at 16 °C. Once completed, it was incubated at 65 °C for 10 min to inactivate the T4 DNA ligase. Subsequently, exonuclease I (5 U/μL), exonuclease III (200 U/μL), and their corresponding buffers were added and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. After completion, it was incubated at 80 °C for 15 min to inactivate the exonucleases. Finally, the circular template was purified by alcohol precipitation and stored at −20 °C until use.

The long single-strand DNA was synthesized using RCA technology, as described in our previous literature [36]. Specifically, a mixture of 2 μL DNA primer (10 μM), 2 μL circular DNA template (10 μM), 2 μL 10 × Phi29 DNA polymerase buffer, 2 μL dNTPs (7 mM), 0.5 μL Phi29 DNA polymerase, and 11.5 μL H2O was prepared. After centrifugation, it reacted at 30 °C for 25 min and was then inactivated at 65 °C for 10 min. The RCA product was obtained upon cooling to room temperature. The concentration of the RCA product was obtained through a Micro-spectrophotometer (Nano-100).

First, mix 4 μL RCA product (0.2 μM) with 7 μL AS1411@R-CDs (4 μM, 2.86 mg/mL), then add 4 μL A2 strand (7 μM), 4 μL A3 strand (7 μM), and 1 μL H2O. Next, add 20 μL 2 × TE-Mg2+ buffer to bring the total volume to 40 μL. Subject the mixture to thermal treatment in a polymerase chain reaction (PCR) machine, starting with a 5 min incubation at 95 °C, followed by gradual cooling at a rate of 1 °C per min down to room temperature to promote the formation of assembly. Finally, purify the obtained Assembly@R-CDs using a centrifugal device and store the purified assembly at 4 °C for future use. As for Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS, Mix Assembly@R-CDs and MnO2 NS and react to obtain Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS.
The details were provided in the “Supplementary information.”

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of R-CDs and MnO2 NS
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesizing R-CDs through aromatic amines had the advantages of being able to adjust the energy band structure and surface state of CDs, shifting the emission wavelength to the red region to promote luminescence, as well as passivating the surface defects of CDs and reducing non-radiative recombination centers to improve the luminous intensity [25,37,38]. Consequently, the synthesis of R-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 A, was achieved through a one-step hydrothermal process using APAA and 4-Br-1,2-DAB in ultrapure water, followed by filtration and dialysis for purification. The TEM (Fig. 1B) image of R-CDs showed that they exhibit excellent dispersibility and a uniform dot-like morphology with an average diameter of 2.67 nm through summarizing more than one hundred particles (Fig. 1C). The XRD image of R-CDs reveals a graphitic structure, characterized by two distinct peaks at approximately 23.0° and 28.0° (Fig. 1D), which corresponded to the interlayer spacing of 3.2 Å and the characteristic diffraction peaks of the (002) plane in a highly crystalline graphite structure [10]. To elucidate the surface composition of R-CDs, a thorough characterization was conducted using FT-IR and XPS. FT-IR (Fig. 1E) of R-CDs appears signals at 618.1, 827.3, and 1314.8 cm−1 were attributed to the C–Br, N–H, and C–C/C–N stretching vibration, respectively [39,40]. The peaks at 1515.7, 1626.3, and 2928.7 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CC/CN, CO, and C–H, respectively [41,42]. The absorbance bands at 3195.1 and 3370.3 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N–H, and O–H, respectively [42]. The XPS data (Fig. 1F) indicated that four peaks were present at 68.39, 284.8, 399.52, and 531.92 eV, which were belonged to Br 3d (15.61 at.%), C 1s (39.58 at.%), N 1s (29.44 at.%), and O 1s (15.37 at.%), respectively. As depicted in Fig. 1G–J shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Br 3d, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s were fitted. In the high-resolution N1s spectrum, the binding energy signals at 68.1 and 70.1 eV, assigning to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively [43]. The signals of high-resolution C 1s at 284.2, 285.1, and 285.8 eV were ascribed to C–H, C–N/C–C, and C–O, respectively [44]. The peaks at 399.2 and 400.9 eV of high-resolution N 1s were ascribed to C–N and N–H, respectively [43]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum was fitted with three peaks at 531.1, 532.2, and 533.5 eV, attributing to CO, C–O, and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively [43,44]. The XPS spectra revealed that R-CDs were composed of the elements Br, C, N, and O, with atomic percentages of 5.5 %, 7.9 %, 14.8 %, and 71.9 %, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. S1. The consistency between the FT-IR and XPS spectra confirms that R-CDs were enriched with hydrophilic groups, which conferred the excellent water solubility to R-CDs and thus enhance their suitability for biological applications.

As shown in Fig. S2A, the TEM image revealed that the prepared MnO2 NS has a relatively uniform sheet-like structure. The XRD image (Fig. S2B) of MnO2 NS indicated that four peaks were present at 12.2, 24.5, 36.8, and 65.7, which were belonged to the 001, 002, 110, and 310 planes [No. 86–06661], respectively [24]. The FT-IR data (Fig. S2C) of MnO2 NS exhibited three characteristic peaks at 672.2, 1406.2, and 3423.1 cm−1, which were analogous to the stretching vibrations of O–Mn–O, O–H, and H–O–H, respectively [45]. The experimental results above indicated that MnO2 NS have been successfully synthesized.

R-CDs were studied through the measurement of fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra, followed by a subsequent investigation into the optical properties of R-CDs. The solution of R-CDs emitted red light under 365 nm UV lamp, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1K, black line) presented distinct absorption bands within the ranges of 220–260 nm and 340–660 nm. These bands were attributed to the π→π∗ and n→π∗ electronic transitions of the aromatic π framework that incorporates carbonyl (CO), nitrile (CN), carbonyl triple bond (CO), and nitrile triple bond (CN) functionalities [46]. Furthermore, the R-CDs demonstrated an optimal emission peak at 657 nm when excited at the wavelength of 535 nm (Fig. S3). It was worth noting that nanomaterials with an emission wavelength exceeding 650 nm could be classified as near-infrared emitting materials [11]. QY was an important indicator for measuring the optical performance of R-CDs, and the optimal QY of 18 % (using Rhodamine B as a reference) was achieved under the following conditions: (a) mole ratio of 4-Bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene to N-(4-aminophenyl)-acetamide of 1:1; (b) reaction time of 8 h; (c) reaction temperature of 180 °C (Fig. S4). Compared with the related literatures that had been reported (Table S2), the R-CDs prepared by us had many advantages. Its synthesis method was convenient, the processing steps were simple, and it had a longer emission wavelength. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained plateau even at high salt concentrations, and did not change significantly within a wide pH range of 4–12 (Figs. S5A and B), indicating that R-CDs could serve as fluorescent probes in various biological environments, including inflammatory and tumor microenvironments. After continuous exposure to a 365 nm UV lamp for 2 h, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs exhibited excellent stability (Fig. S5C), indicating their good photostability, which could address the poor stability and easy decomposition issues of traditional fluorescent dyes like 6-Carboxyfluorescein Amidite (FAM). Additionally, R-CDs still exhibit good fluorescence under various metal ions (Fig. S6). The spontaneous fluorescence interference in biological tissues was typically blue-green, and the high QY red fluorescence of R-CDs helped avoid this interference, making them suitable for deep tissue and in vivo biological imaging. As a whole, due to their stability and high QY, R-CDs were a kind of promising fluorescent labeling material in the field of biological imaging [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of determining GSH using the R-CDs@MnO2 NS nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
MnO2 NS exhibited a broad absorption spectrum in the UV to visible light region (300 nm–700 nm), which significantly overlapped with the excitation and emission bands of R-CDs (Fig. 2 A). Therefore, it was preliminarily inferred that the fluorescence quenching mechanism may involve Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) or the inner filter effect (IFE) [23]. A notable characteristic of the FRET mechanism was that the UV–vis absorption spectrum simultaneously displayed the absorption peaks of both the donor and the acceptor, with the donor's peak corresponding to its excited state and the acceptor's peak corresponding to its ground state [47]. As shown in Fig. 2A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs@MnO2 NS displayed distinct peaks for both the donor and acceptor, which further supported the hypothesis of the FRET quenching mechanism. Fluorescence lifetime measurement, serving as a robust indicator of energy transfer processes, revealed a reduction in the donor molecule's fluorescence lifetime upon energy transferred to the acceptor molecule. In this study, when MnO2 NS were mixed with R-CDs, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was observed to decrease significantly from 1.71 ns to 1.24 ns (Fig. 2B), which was in line with the expected effect of the FRET mechanism. FRET not only required significant spectral overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum, which was a necessary condition for energy transfer, but also typically required the donor and acceptor to be within a distance of 10 nm of each other. Zeta potential measurements indicated that the potential of R-CDs was +16.9 mV, while that of MnO2 NS was −38.9 mV (Fig. 2C). This potential difference promoted electrostatic complexation between R-CDs and MnO2 NS, thereby shortening the distance between them and enhancing the FRET. TEM images (Fig. 2D) revealed that the MnO2 NS alone exhibit a flaky structure, and their hydrodynamic diameter (Fig. 2G) was slightly larger than that shown in the TEM images, which may be due to surface adsorption, particle aggregation, and hydration expansion effects of the nanomaterials in aqueous solutions. When MnO2 NS were mixed with R-CDs, the particle size slightly increased (Fig. 2E–H), and the addition of GSH leads to the decomposition of MnO2 NS, leaving only R-CDs (Fig. 2F–I). As depicted in Fig. S7, MnO2 NS did not exhibit fluorescence. However, when MnO2 NS were incorporated into R-CDs, the fluorescence of R-CDs was quenched, and the addition of GSH restored the quenched fluorescence. Additionally, the XRD image (Fig. S8) showed that R-CDs@MnO2 NS included characteristic peaks of both R-CDs and MnO2 NS, and the characteristic absorption peaks of MnO2 NS almost disappeared after the addition of GSH. Because GSH could cause the decomposition of MnO2 NS, preventing the quenching effect on R-CDs. In this process, MnO2 was reduced to Mn2+ while GSH itself was oxidized to glutathione disulfide (GSSG), as shown in the following equation (1).
(1) MnO2+GSH+2H+ → Mn2++GSSG+2H2O

Through FTIR analysis, we further explored whether R-CDs and MnO2 NS could stably bind. The FTIR image (Fig. S9) showed characteristic absorption peaks at wavenumbers 3421 and 1413 cm−1, which belonged to the O–H groups of MnO2 NS, further proving the close binding of MnO2 NS with R-CDs [45]. In summary, the experimental results indicated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of R-CDs was primarily due to FRET, and the detection of GSH using R-CDs@MnO2 NS was feasible.

To achieve high-sensitivity detection of GSH, we conducted a preliminary exploration of various experimental conditions. As depicted in Fig. S10, the fluorescence quenched and remained plateau after the addition of MnO2 NS for 1 min. Fig. S11A, B revealed that the optimal detection effect was achieved when the concentrations of R-CDs and MnO2 NS were set to 50 μg/mL and 25 μg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. S11C indicated that the highest fluorescence recovery efficiency of R-CDs was attained in PBS buffer solution with a pH value of 7.4. Fig. S11D demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs@MnO2 NS composite increases after the introduction of GSH and stabilizes after 10 min. These optimized conditions obtained above will be adopted in the subsequent experiments. In Fig. 3 A, it was evident that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs@MnO2 NS exhibited a direct proportional increase as the concentration of GSH ranges from 0 to 800 μM. Furthermore, Fig. 3B illustrated a linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs@MnO2 NS and GSH concentration within the range of 0.5–120 μM. Utilizing the formula LOD = 3σ/S, where σ was the relative standard deviation of 11 blank samples and S was the slope of the calibration curve, we have determined the LOD to be 0.01 μM. The linear equation was Y = 0.015C GSH + 1.06 (R 2 = 0.994). Compared with other GSH detection methods reported in the literature (as shown in Table S3), it was evident that our method was comparable to existing techniques and offers significant competitive advantages. The measurement results not only confirmed its efficacy but also highlighted its potential for practical applications in real-world scenarios.

As shown in Fig. 3C, the selectivity of the R-CDs@MnO2 nanoprobe for GSH detection was tested against various amino acids and biomolecules, including L-Tyr, L-Cys, L-Leu, L-Arg, L-Pro, and others. The results showed that most interferents had little to no effect on the nanoprobe's fluorescence recovery rate. Even though L-Cys and AA at 1 mM could cause slight changes, the typical millimolar concentration of GSH in biological systems is much higher than the micromolar levels of these interferents [18], suggesting minimal interference. Further tests confirmed that the nanoprobe could accurately detect GSH in the presence of other coexisting analytes (Fig. 3D) without significant interference.

Under normal circumstances, cells lack specificity in the uptake of free CDs, which limited their application in precise targeting for medical treatment. To overcome this limitation, specific targeting of cancer cells was achieved, and the application of R-CDs in detecting GSH within biological matrices was further expanded by conjugating AS1411 with R-CDs. Considering that DNA typically carries a negative charge and R-CDs carry a positive charge, they could easily combine through electrostatic interactions. To further enhance the conjugation efficiency, chemical methods were also employed for assistance. Specifically, EDC and NHS were used to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs, enabling them to react with amine-modified DNA to form amide bonds. As shown in Fig. S12, AS1411 exhibits a typical UV–visible absorbance peak at 256 nm. After conjugation with R-CDs, the absorbance peak of R-CDs shifts from 239 nm to 257 nm, indicating the successful conjugation of AS1411 to R-CDs [48]. RCA, as an isothermal enzymatic reaction, could produce a large number of repetitive unit linear DNA in a short period of time. Using the RCA product as a scaffold, an assembly containing three different short chains was constructed, one of which was A2 chain containing AS1411 aptamer that could specifically target the nucleolus of the target cells, forming multivalent aptamers with superior targeting capabilities compared to monovalent aptamer. The assembly process was detected by agarose gel electrophoresis. In the pre-assembly experiment, the binding of one repetitive unit (63 base sequences) of the RCA product to the 3 short chains was tested. As shown in Fig. S13A, with the addition of each short chain, the brightness and molecular weight of the bands gradually increased, indicating that the assembly process was feasible. In the subsequent RCA self-assembly experiment (Fig. S13B), with the addition of different concentrations of short chains, the assembly product blocked at the well, indicating the formation of high molecular weight products, which also confirmed the success of the RCA assembly. The molar ratio of RCA chains to functional short chains was optimized, and it was found that when the RCA to short chain concentration ratio was 1:40, the assembly efficiency was the highest. Considering both assembly efficiency and cost, this concentration ratio was adopted for subsequent experiments. The results of gel electrophoresis also demonstrated that AS1411@R-CDs could assemble with RCA to form Assembly@R-CDs. Due to the positive charge of R-CDs, to prevent the assembly from being dragged out of the gel during electrophoresis, the well was placed in the middle of the gel. As shown in Fig. S14A, the A2 short chain within the assembly without R-CDs conjugation remained at the well, while the A2 short chain within the assembly conjugated with R-CDs (Assembly@R-CDs) moved in the opposite direction of the electric field due to the charge of the R-CDs. This phenomenon was also confirmed by the sunlight image (Fig. S14B), fully demonstrating the successful assembly of Assembly@R-CDs.

To ensure the biocompatibility of R-CDs and MnO2 NS for biomedical applications, cytotoxicity tests were performed using CCK-8 assay on L929 and 4T1 cells. After 24 and 48 h, cell survival exceeded 80 % even at 200 μg/mL R-CDs, indicating low cytotoxicity (Figs. S15A and B). Notably, R-CDs showed better compatibility with 4T1 cancer cells than L929 normal cells. MnO2 NS also showed good biocompatibility (Figs. S15C and D). Further tests using Annexin V-FITC/PI staining and flow cytometry confirmed low cytotoxicity of Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS, with no significant apoptosis or necrosis observed after 12–48 h of incubation (Fig. S16). This low toxicity is crucial for their use in live cell imaging and biosensing.

R-CDs, with their excellent optical properties and biocompatibility, were highly suitable for applications such as cell imaging. As shown in Fig. S17, since the auto fluorescence in biological bodies was typically blue-green, after incubating R-CDs with L929 and 4T1 cells for 8 h, a strong red fluorescence could be observed, indicating that R-CDs have good resistance to interference from spontaneous fluorescence. It was well known that the AS1411 aptamer could specifically bind to nucleolin and rapidly target tumor cells. Especially in 4T1 cells, the expression of nucleolin was several orders of magnitude higher than in L929 cells, making 4T1 cells an ideal target for the AS1411 aptamer. Therefore, the potential of AS1411@R-CDs in bioimaging and targeting 4T1 cells was explored. Fluorescence imaging using an inverted fluorescence microscope was performed to assess the cellular internalization efficiency of AS1411@R-CDs. After incubation for 30 min, the internalization effect of AS1411@R-CDs was comparable to that of R-CDs incubated for 4 h alone (Fig. S18), indicating the potential of AS1411@R-CDs to rapidly target cancer cells. In addition, the rapid targeting capability of AS1411@R-CDs in L929 and 4T1 cells was evaluated through FL imaging and flow cytometry analysis. As shown in Fig. 4 A, compared with normal cells L929, 4T1 cells showed significant internalization and red fluorescence after incubation with AS1411@R-CDs for 30 min, while L929 cells exhibited much weaker fluorescence. Colocalization analysis with ImageJ (Fig. 4C) and flow cytometry results (Fig. 4B) both support this finding, indicating the potential of AS1411@R-CDs to differentiate between tumor cells and normal cells. After incubation for 30 min with R-CDs, AS1411@R-CDs and Assembly@R-CDs respectively, the corresponding FL images and flow cytometry images (Fig. 4D) showed that the cells incubated with Assembly@R-CDs have a stronger fluorescence compared to R-CDs and AS1411@R-CDs, indicating that Assembly@R-CDs have a higher degree of internalization. The rapid targeting of Assembly@R-CDs may be attributed to its role as a multivalent adapter capable of carrying more AS1411 aptamer, thus possessing a higher affinity for nucleolin receptors [32,33]. Additionally, the small size and strong cell membrane permeability of R-CDs, along with the positive charge they carried, made smaller positively charged substances more likely to penetrate the typically negatively charged cell membrane. This was due to their larger surface area to volume ratio, which facilitated easier interaction with the cell membrane, thereby enhancing internalization and targeting capabilities [49,50]. Experimental results suggest that the targeting effect was performed under such optimal conditions of (a) R-CDs: 50 μg/mL (Fig. S19), (b) incubation time: 30 min (Fig. S20), and (c) the concentration of the AS1411 aptamer: 0.07 μM (Fig. S21).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing the feasibility of cellular GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its excellent biocompatibility, Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS was considered an ideal candidate for detecting GSH in 4T1 cells in vitro. We explored the imaging and monitoring of intracellular GSH changes using live 4T1 cells. To assess the potential impact of free thiol (-SH) compounds on the fluorescence recovery of the assembly, we introduced NEM as an alkylating agent that reacts with free thiol groups to form stable thioether bonds, thereby effectively inhibiting compounds such as GSH. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence recovery of the assembly gradually decreased with increasing NEM concentration, almost disappearing at 500 μM. Based on this result, a 500 μM concentration of NEM was selected to pretreat 4T1 cells. After pretreatment, as the GSH concentration increased from 100 μM to 800 μM, the fluorescence intensity of 4T1 cells increased, as shown in Fig. 5B. To evaluate the selectivity of this method, we studied whether certain amino acids could restore fluorescence. As shown in Fig. S22, 4T1 cells were pretreated with NEM. After adding 1 mM amino acid, there was almost no change in intracellular fluorescence. These results indicated that the assembly was non-toxic to living cells and could selectively detect GSH within cells without interference from other components of the biological matrix.

The ability of Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS to rapidly target and detect GSH in cancer cells has inspired us to further explore its application in cancer cell imaging. It was well known that the GSH content in tumor cells was usually much higher than in normal cells, and there were also differences in GSH content among different cancer cells. Therefore, Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS, serving as a GSH detection probe, could distinguish between cancer cells and normal cells based on their differing GSH concentrations. In cell recognition experiments, we used FL imaging technology to study the response of Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS in live 4T1 cancer cells and L929 normal cells. Since the optimal reaction time for R-CDs and MnO2 NS was 10 min, therefore, to achieve the best cancer cell recognition effect, the corresponding incubation time should be increased (Fig. S11D). Fig. 6 A showed that as the incubation time increased, the 4T1 cells co-incubated with Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS exhibited gradually enhanced red fluorescence, while the red fluorescence of L929 cells was negligible. This indicated that Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS could indeed specifically recognize cancer cells through the high expression of GSH. In addition, we measured the total fluorescence intensity of 4T1 and L929 cells after co-incubation with Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS for different periods by flow cytometry, respectively. As described in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of 4T1 cells co-incubated with Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS increased with time, while the fluorescence intensity of L929 cells remained almost unchanged. These results conclusively demonstrated that Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS could be used for monitoring of intracellular GSH levels, thereby rapidly and specifically identifying cancer cells.

In summary, a long-wavelength R-CDs that exhibiting efficient red fluorescence emission with QY of 18 % was successfully synthesized through the hydrothermal method. A novel sensing platform, designated as Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS, was constructed utilizing RCA technology for bioimaging identification cancer cells from normal cells via sensing GSH. Specifically, Assembly@R-CDs exhibited significantly higher internalization in 4T1 cells compared to standalone R-CDs and AS1411@R-CDs, demonstrating the potential for rapid targeting of tumor cells. The addition of MnO2 NS quenched the fluorescence of R-CDs, resulting in the formation of non-fluorescent Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS. After treating the cells with NEM to eliminate intracellular thiol compounds, followed by the addition of GSH, the originally quenched fluorescence of Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS was restored, confirming the platform's ability to achieve fluorescence imaging recognition of tumor cells. Fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry analysis indicated that the designed Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS could serve as a fluorescent probe to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells by detecting the overexpression of GSH concentration. Due to its good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, Assembly@R-CDs@MnO2 NS was poised to become a new type of multifunctional nanomaterial, highly suitable for the integration of diagnosis and therapy as well as other biomedical applications, offering new avenues for future biomedical research.

Gan Ning: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft preparation. Fang Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Huan Du: Methodology. Ruyan Zhang: Methodology. Xiaobing Huo: Methodology. Xiufeng Wang: Project administration. Ting Zhou: Project administration. Guodong Zhang: Data curation. Zhiqing Zhang: Resources, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022MB148 and ZR2023MB148) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (22CX03024A, 23CX03005A and 23CX03006A).

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Topological single‐stranded DNA encoding and programmable assembly of molecular nanostructures for NIR‐II cancer theranostics
